Football context should enhance, not overwhelm\n- **Football-Inspired but Subtle**: Colors and imagery reference football without being cartoonish\n- **Accessible & Readable**: High contrast ratios, sans-serif;\n```\n(Tailwind default is fine)\n\n### Hierarchy\n- **Page Titles (h1)**: \n  - Size: `2.25rem` (36px) ��� Tailwind `text-4xl`\n  - Weight: `700` (bold)\n  - Color: `#f1f5f9`\n  - Margin: `#f1f5f9`\n  - Line Height: `1.6`\n\n- **Small/Metadata**:\n  - Size: `0.875rem` (14px) ��� Tailwind `text-sm`\n  - Color: `#cbd5e1`\n\n---\n\n## Layout & Spacing\n\n### Grid System\n- Use Tailwind's **12-column grid** for consistency\n- **Container max-width**: `max-w-7xl` (80rem)\n- **Padding**: \n  - Page/container: `2rem` (32px) padding on all sides\n  - Card internal: `1.5rem` (24px)\n  - Between sections: `2-3rem` (32-48px gap)\n\n### Responsive Breakpoints (use Tailwind defaults)\n- Mobile: base (no prefix)\n- Tablet: `md:` (768px)\n- Desktop: `lg:` (1024px)\n- Large: `xl:` (1280px)\n\n### Spacing Unit\nAll spacing uses multiples of `0.25rem` (4px):\n- `p-2` = 8px, `p-4` = 16px, `p-6` = 24px, `m-4` = 16px, etc.\n\n---\n\n## Components\n\n### Navigation Bar transition-colors`\n  - Active: `text-emerald-400 border-b-2 border-emerald-400`\n- **Dropdown/Mobile menu** (if responsive): Dark background, same color scheme\n\n### Cards (for lists/index pages)\n```css\n.card {\n  background: #1e293b;         /* bg-slate-800 */\n  border: 1px solid #334155;   /* border-slate-700 */\n  border-radius: 0.5rem;       /* rounded-lg */\n  padding: 1.5rem; Text: `text-slate-50`\n  - Padding: `px-3 py-2`\n  - Rounded: `rounded-md`\n  - Focus: `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-emerald-500 focus:border-emerald-500`\n\n### Alert/Message Boxes\n- **Success**: `bg-emerald-900 text-emerald-100 border border-emerald-700`\n- **Error**: `bg-red-900 text-red-100 border border-red-700`\n- **Warning**: `bg-amber-900 text-amber-100 border border-amber-700`\n- **Info**: `bg-blue-900 text-blue-100 border border-blue-700`\n\n---\n\n## Page-Specific Guidelines\n\n### Dashboard / Home Page\n- **Hero Section**: Large title with subtitle, background gradient (dark slate to slightly lighter)\n- **Stats Cards**: 3-4 key stats (total matches, top player, recent score) in grid layout\n- **Recent Activity**: Last 5-10 matches in a timeline or compact table\n- **League Overview**: Small table or cards showing league standings\n- **Call-to-Action**: Prominent link to browse players/matches\n\n### Index Pages (Players, Clubs, Matches, Ratings, Users)\n- **Page Header**: Title + count of items\n- **Filter/Search Bar** (if added later): Above the list\n- **Cards or Table**: Display all items, clickable rows/cards link to Details\n- **Pagination** (optional): If more than 10 items\n\n### Details Pages\n- **Header Section**: Large title with badge/status\n- **Key Information**: 2-3 columns of data with clear labels\n- **Related Data**: Links to related entities (e.g., matches for a player, players for a club)\n- **Breadcrumb Navigation**: `Home > Entities > Entity Name`\n- **Action Links**: Link back to list, link to related entities\n\n---\n\n## Interaction & Transitions\n\n- **Hover States**: Subtle color shift or border color change (300ms transition)\n- **Focus States**: Ring outline (2px) in accent color\n- **Active States**: Bolder text or underline for navigation\n- **Loading States** (if applicable): Spinner or skeleton loaders with dark background\n- **Smooth Transitions**: Use `transition-all duration-300` for most interactions\n\n---\n\n## Dark Mode Do's & Don'ts\n\n### ԣ� DO\n- Use sufficient contrast (WCAG AA minimum 4.5:1 for normal text)\n- Include subtle borders/dividers to separate sections\n- Use color accents sparingly (emerald for primary actions)\n- Make clickable elements obvious (hover/focus states)\n- Keep backgrounds consistently dark (don't mix too many shades)\n\n### ��� DON'T\n- Use pure black (`#000000`) ��� too harsh; use `#0f172a` or `#1e293b`\n- Use pure white (`#ffffff`) ��� too bright; use `#f1f5f9`\n- Overuse ornaments or images; keep it clean\n- Hide interactive elements (buttons, links must be obvious)\n- Use very small fonts; minimum `text-base` for body content\n- Mix too many accent colors (emerald is primary; others are supporting)\n\n---\n\n## Implementation Strategy for Sub-Agent\n\nWhen invoking this UX sub-agent, provide:\n\n1. **Specific Page/Component**: \"Update the player index view to use cards instead of a table\"\n2. **Context**: CSS\n5. **Constraints**: Any restrictions on libraries or file locations\n\n### Example Prompt for UX Sub-Agent:\n```\nUpdate Views/Shared/_Layout.cshtml:\n- Replace Bootstrap navbar with a building):\n```html\n<script>\n  tailwind.config = {\n    theme: {\n      extend: {\n        colors: {\n          'slate': { 50: '#f8fafc', 100: '#f1f5f9', /* ... existing tailwind colors ... */, 900: '#0f172a' }\n        }\n      }\n    }\n  }\n</script>\n```\n\n---\n\n## Git & Documentation\n\nThis instruction file should be:\n1. **Committed to Git** as `UX_AGENT_INSTRUCTIONS.md`\n2. **Referenced Include keywords that help agents identify relevant tasks.\n+description: Use this skill when defining UI and UX direction for an ASP.NET Core MVC app, planning a design system, interviewing the user about layout and visual preferences, creating a UX brief, identifying reusable components, or deciding how list, details, and form pages should look and behave. Keywords: UI, UX, design system, layout, dashboard, admin panel, list page, details page, form, cards, tables, navigation, responsive, Razor views, MVC.\n ---\n \n <!-- Tip: Use /create-skill in chat to generate content with agent assistance -->\n \n-Define the functionality provided by this skill, including detailed instructions and examples\n+# UI/UX Discovery\n+\n+Use this skill before generating UI code when the project does not yet have a clear visual or interaction direction.\n+\n+This skill helps the agent:\n+- interview the user about desired UI and UX direction\n+- inspect the existing MVC structure when needed\n+- identify reusable page patterns\n+- produce a concise UX brief\n+- prepare future UI skills for list, details, and form pages\n+\n+This skill is for discovery and planning first. It should not jump straight into implementation unless the user explicitly asks for code.\n+\n+## Use This Skill When\n+\n+Use this skill when:\n+- the app does not yet have a clear UI direction\n+- the user says they want to \"play with UI/UX ideas\" before coding\n+- the user wants a design system or reusable page patterns\n+- the user wants help choosing between dashboard, admin, sports, editorial, or minimal styles\n+- the user wants to standardize list pages, details pages, or forms\n+- the user wants the agent to ask questions first instead of inventing a design\n+\n+## Do Not Use This Skill When\n+\n+Do not use this skill when:\n+- the user already gave a complete visual brief and wants direct implementation\n+- the task is purely backend, EF, routing, or database related\n+- the user only wants a one-off CSS tweak with no design discussion\n+- the user asks for debugging unrelated UI code behavior\n+\n+## Primary Goal\n+\n+Your job is to turn vague UI wishes into a practical design brief that fits the existing ASP.NET Core MVC application.\n+\n+You must:\n+- ask a short, focused discovery interview\n+- separate confirmed preferences from your own recommendations\n+- keep recommendations grounded in the current project structure\n+- identify reusable components and page archetypes\n+- leave implementation for a later step unless explicitly requested\n+\n+## Discovery Workflow\n+\n+Follow this order.\n+\n+### 1. Understand Product Intent\n+\n+First determine what kind of application the user wants.\n+\n+Ask about:\n+- who the main user is\n+- what the most important tasks are\n+- whether the app should feel like an admin panel, sports dashboard, data browser, or showcase experience\n+- whether speed of use or visual presentation matters more\n+\n+### 2. Clarify Visual Direction\n+\n+Ask about:\n+- dark, light, or mixed theme\n+- compact or spacious layout\n+- tables or cards for list pages\n+- minimal or expressive design\n+- subtle or strong domain branding\n+- reference apps, screenshots, or styles they like\n+\n+### 3. Clarify Interaction Patterns\n+\n+Ask about:\n+- mobile priority versus desktop priority\n+- whether details pages should be compact or richer and more visual\n+- whether filters, search, badges, breadcrumbs, and stat cards are desirable\n+- whether forms should be standard CRUD forms or more guided experiences\n+\n+### 4. Inspect Existing Structure If Helpful\n+\n+If the workspace already contains views, layout files, or CSS, inspect them and align your recommendations with reality.\n+\n+Prefer checking:\n+- shared layout\n+- index pages\n+- details pages\n+- existing nav structure\n+- styling conventions\n+- current use of Bootstrap, Tailwind, CSS files, or partials\n+\n+### 5. Synthesize a UX Brief\n+\n+After the interview, produce a structured brief with these sections:\n+- Product Direction\n+- Users and Main Tasks\n+- Visual Direction\n+- Page Archetypes\n+- Reusable Components\n+- Interaction Principles\n+- Constraints\n+- Open Questions\n+- Recommended Next Step\n+\n+## Output Requirements\n+\n+Your output should be concise and structured.\n+\n+Always distinguish:\n+- Confirmed Preferences\n+- Inferred Recommendations\n+- Open Questions\n+\n+Avoid long generic design lectures.\n+\n+Prefer actionable conclusions like:\n+- use card-based details pages with stat blocks\n+- keep list pages table-first on desktop\n+- use consistent action bars across index and details pages\n+- standardize badges for domain labels like league or player position\n+\n+## Design Heuristics\n+\n+When making recommendations:\n+- prefer consistency over novelty\n+- respect the constraints of Razor views and MVC page composition\n+- recommend reusable partials when the same UI block will appear on multiple pages\n+- avoid proposing SPA-like interaction unless the user clearly wants it\n+- do not invent animations, themes, or layouts without user intent\n+- do not overload CRUD pages with visual noise\n+\n+## Expected Page Archetypes\n+\n+When relevant, organize findings around these page types:\n+- Dashboard\n+- List or Index Page\n+- Details Page\n+- Create or Edit Form\n+- Shared Layout and Navigation\n+\n+For each archetype, suggest:\n+- its job\n+- its information hierarchy\n+- the most suitable layout pattern\n+- which components between conventional routing and attribute routing\n+- design custom URLs that still fit MVC conventions\n+- update routing with the smallest safe change\n+- document the reachable URLs in skill when:\n+- the user wants a custom URL instead of the default `/Controller/Action/{id?}` pattern\n+- the user wants to add or refactor `MapControllerRoute(...)` definitions in `Program.cs`\n+- the user wants to use `[Route(...)]` on a controller or action\n+- the user wants to document all current URLs in `sitemap.md`\n+- the lab requires at least 4 custom-routed controller actions\n+- the task is mainly frontend Razor layout or CSS work\n+- the task is only about controller business logic with no URL change\n+- the user only wants a general explanation of MVC with no routing change or analysis\n+\n+## Primary Goal\n+\n+Your job is to produce correct, testable, documented routing behavior.\n+\n+You must:\n+- identify the current routing entry point before editing\n+- decide where the route should live: `Program.cs` or attributes\n+- preserve existing behavior unless the user asked to replace it\n+- verify which URL maps to which controller and action\n+- keep `sitemap.md` aligned with the implemented routes when documentation is part of the task\n+\n+## Routing Workflow\n+\n+Follow this order.\n+\n+### 1. Inspect Current Routing Surface\n+\n+First inspect:\n+- `Program.cs`\n+- the target controller\n+- the target action signatures\n+- the relevant view paths under `Views/<Controller>/`\n+\n+Determine:\n+- whether the app currently relies only on the default conventional route\n+- whether custom routes already exist\n+- whether the target action already has route attributes\n+- whether route parameters match the action parameters\n+\n+### 2. Define the Routing Intent\n+\n+Clarify what the route should achieve.\n+\n+Capture:\n+- target URL pattern\n+- which controller and action should handle it\n+- whether the route is public-facing, semantic, localized, or admin-oriented\n+- whether route parameters are optional, required, or constrained\n+- whether the route should coexist with the default route or replace it for that action\n+\n+If details are vague, ask focused questions rather than guessing.\n+\n+### 3. Choose the Routing Style\n+\n+Prefer the simplest routing mechanism that fits the requirement.\n+\n+Use conventional routing in `Program.cs` when:\n+- several routes follow a shared pattern\n+- the route is app-level and easier to understand centrally\n+- the user wants a route table style setup\n+\n+Use attribute routing when:\n+- the route is tightly coupled to one controller or action\n+- the URL should be obvious next to the action code\n+- different actions in the same controller need distinct semantic URLs\n+\n+Do not mix both styles for the same route unless there is a clear reason.\n+\n+### 4. Design the Route Carefully\n+\n+Before editing, check:\n+- parameter names match the action signature\n+- optional parameters are truly optional in the action\n+- constraints reflect real requirements\n+- route order will not shadow or break more specific routes\n+- controller and view naming still follow MVC conventions\n+\n+When relevant, prefer semantic URLs such as:\n+- `/lige/premier-league`\n+- `/utakmice/123`\n+- `/igraci/8/ocjene`\n+\n+### 5. Implement the Smallest Safe Change\n+\n+When editing:\n+- add the custom route with the narrowest needed scope\n+- preserve the default route unless the task explicitly removes it\n+- avoid broad rewrites of unrelated controllers\n+- keep action names, parameters, and views aligned\n+- update links or `asp-action`/`asp-controller` usage if the route strategy requires it\n+\n+### 6. Validate the Route\n+\n+After the change, validate at least these points:\n+- the project builds\n+- the intended URL resolves to the expected action\n+- parameters bind correctly\n+- no previously working route was broken unintentionally\n+\n+If executable validation is available, prefer it over reasoning alone.\n+\n+### 7. Document the Routing Model\n+\n+When the task includes documentation, update or create `sitemap.md`.\n+\n+For each reachable URL include:\n+- URL pattern\n+- controller\n+- action\n+- route style: conventional or attribute\n+- expected view file\n+- key parameters and constraints if any\n+\n+Keep the document concise and factual.\n+\n+## Lab-Focused Completion Checks\n+\n+For this lab, treat the routing task as complete only if these checks pass:\n+- at least 4 custom-routed controller actions exist\n+- those 4 are not counted only through the default route\n+- the route definitions and action signatures are consistent\n+- `sitemap.md` reflects the implemented URLs\n+- the app still has a valid fallback/default navigation path unless the user asked otherwise\n+\n+## Output Requirements\n+\n+When answering or implementing, always distinguish:\n+- current routing state\n+- planned routing change\n+- validation result\n+- documentation impact\n+\n+Avoid vague statements like \"routing updated\".\n+\n+Prefer concrete summaries such as:\n+- added attribute routes for `MatchController.Details` and `PlayerController.Details`\n+- added named conventional routes in `Program.cs` for league and rating pages\n+- documented every reachable page in `sitemap.md`\n+\n+## Good Behavior\n+\n+Good behavior looks like this:\n+- inspect `Program.cs` before editing routes\n+- confirm the action method signature matches route parameters\n+- use route constraints only when they add real value\n+- keep custom URLs semantic and predictable\n+- document the final route mapping clearly\n+\n+## Bad Behavior\n+\n+Bad behavior looks like this:\n+- adding route attributes without checking for conflicts\n+- changing route patterns without verifying links and parameters\n+- counting default-route access as a custom route for the lab\n+- mixing route strategies randomly across the same feature\n+- documenting routes that do not actually exist in code\n+\n+## Example Prompts\n+\n+Use this skill for prompts like:\n+- Add 4 custom routes to this ASP.NET Core MVC app for the lab\n+- Help me choose between attribute routing and Audits the requirement .md file against the current codebase, reports what is satisfied, what is missing, what is unclear, and what can be improved.\n+tools: [read, search]\n+user-invocable: true\n+argument-hint: Provide the lab or project markdown file to audit, plus any scope note such as 'check only routing' or 'full submission readiness'.\n+agents: []\n+---\n+\n+You are a focused lab and project requirements audit agent.\n+\n+Your job is to read a markdown requirements file, inspect the current workspace, and judge how well the implementation satisfies those requirements. You do not just say pass or fail. You identify what is complete, what is partial, what is missing, what is ambiguous, and what could still be improved before submission.\n+\n+## Use This Agent For\n+\n+- checking whether a lab, homework, or project satisfies requirements listed in a `.md` file\n+- auditing submission readiness against explicit acceptance criteria\n+- comparing implemented routing, EF, views, models, docs, or skills against the written task\n+- identifying missing deliverables such as `sitemap.md`, `semantic-model.md`, migrations, or customizations\n+- producing a concrete improvement list before submission\n+\n+## Do Not Use This Agent For\n+\n+- writing the implementation directly\n+- doing broad code review unrelated to the requirement file\n+- guessing project goals without first reading the provided markdown brief\n+- auditing runtime behavior that cannot be inferred from the available files\n+\n+## Constraints\n+\n+- Always start from the requirement markdown file, not from assumptions.\n+- Prefer explicit evidence from files over inference.\n+- Separate `Satisfied`, `Partially satisfied`, `Missing`, and `Unclear`.\n+- If evidence is incomplete, say exactly what is missing.\n+- Give practical improvement advice, not generic encouragement.\n+- Do not claim a requirement is satisfied unless the code or documentation supports it.\n+\n+## Audit Approach\n+\n+1. Read the target markdown file and extract the concrete requirements, deliverables, and completion conditions.\n+2. Group the requirements into checkable categories such as EF, routing, views, documentation, skills, migrations, or project structure.\n+3. Inspect the current workspace only as needed to confirm or refute each requirement.\n+4. Mark each requirement as `Satisfied`, `Partially satisfied`, `Missing`, or `Unclear`.\n+5. For every non-satisfied or weak area, explain what evidence is missing and what should be improved.\n+6. End with the single most important next step for submission readiness.\n+\n+## Output Format\n+\n+Return a concise audit with these sections:\n+\n+- Requirement File\n+- Scope Checked\n+- Satisfied\n+- Partially Satisfied\n+- Missing\n+- Unclear\n+- Improvement Opportunities\n+- Recommended Next Step\n+- Evidence Files\n+\n+## Output Rules\n+\n+- Tie conclusions to real files whenever possible.\n+- Prefer short factual bullets.\n+- Quote or paraphrase the requirement before judging it.\n+- If something looks correct but is not fully proven, mark it `Partially satisfied` or `Unclear`.\n+- Improvement suggestions should be specific and actionable.\n+- Recommend only one next step at the end.\n+\n+## Example Tasks\n+\n+- Check whether this lab project satisfies the requirements from `Lab3.md`.\n+- Audit my submission readiness against the assignment markdown.\n+- Compare the codebase to the project brief and tell me what is still missing.\n+- Read the lab instructions and tell me what I can improve before handing it in.\n+- Verify whether routing, EF, and documentation requirements are actually satisfied.\n*** End Patch"},"tool_use_id":"call_50o6DbSBC59D93aN9sn2U1Cc__vscode-1777798830749","cwd":"c:\\Users\\Fran\\Desktop\\C#\\FutScores"}

It lists the main entities, their primary properties, and the relationships between them.\n+\n+## Entity Overview\n+\n+### League\n+\n+Represents a football competition such as Premier League or La Liga.\n+\n+Main properties:\n+- `Id` - primary key\n+- `Name` - league name\n+\n+Navigation properties:\n+- `Clubs` - clubs that belong to the league\n+- `Matches` - matches played in the league\n+\n+### Club\n+\n+Represents a football club.\n+\n+Main properties:\n+- `Id` - primary key\n+- `Name` - club name\n+- `FoundedDate` - foundation date\n+- `LeagueId` - foreign key to League\n+\n+Navigation properties:\n+- `League` - parent league\n+- `Players` - players currently assigned to the club\n+- `HomeMatches` - matches where the club is the home team\n+- `AwayMatches` - matches where the club is the away team\n+\n+### Player\n+\n+Represents an individual football player.\n+\n+Main properties:\n+- `Id` - primary key\n+- `FirstName` - player's first name\n+- `LastName` - player's last name\n+- `DateOfBirth` - player's date of birth\n+- `Position` - enum value: `Goalkeeper`, `Defender`, `Midfielder`, `Forward`\n+- `ClubId` - foreign key to Club\n+- `Nationality` - player's nationality\n+\n+Navigation properties:\n+- `Club` - club the player belongs to\n+- `Ratings` - ratings given to the player in specific matches\n+\n+### Match\n+\n+Represents a football match between two clubs.\n+\n+Main properties:\n+- `Id` - primary key\n+- `LeagueId` - foreign key to League\n+- `HomeTeamId` - foreign key to Club, home team\n+- `AwayTeamId` - foreign key to Club, away team\n+- `Date` - date of the match\n+- `HomeGoals` - number of goals scored by the home team\n+- `AwayGoals` - number of goals scored by the away team\n+\n+Navigation properties:\n+- `League` - league in which the match was played\n+- `HomeTeam` - club playing at home\n+- `AwayTeam` - club playing away\n+- `Ratings` - player ratings associated with the match\n+\n+### Rating\n+\n+Represents a user-submitted score and optional comment for a player's performance in a match.\n+\n+Main properties:\n+- `Id` - primary key\n+- `PlayerId` - foreign key to Player\n+- `MatchId` - foreign key to Match\n+- `UserId` - foreign key to User\n+- `Score` - integer score from 1 to 10\n+- `Comment` - optional textual comment\n+\n+Navigation properties:\n+- `Player` - rated player\n+- `Match` - match for which the rating was submitted\n+- `User` - user who submitted the rating\n+\n+### User\n+\n+Represents an application user who can submit ratings.\n+\n+Main properties:\n+- `Id` - primary key\n+- `FirstName` - user's first name\n+- `LastName` - user's last name\n+- `Email` - user's email address\n+\n+Navigation properties:\n+- `Ratings` - ratings created by the user\n+\n+## Relationship Summary\n+\n+- `League` 1-N `Club`\n+One league can contain many clubs. Each club belongs to exactly one league.\n+\n+- `League` 1-N `Match`\n+One league can contain many matches. Each match belongs to exactly one league.\n+\n+- `Club` 1-N `Player`\n+One club can have many players. Each player belongs to exactly one club.\n+\n+- `Club` 1-N `Match` as home team\n+One club can appear in many matches as `HomeTeam`.\n+\n+- `Club` 1-N `Match` as away team\n+One club can appear in many matches as `AwayTeam`.\n+\n+- `Player` 1-N `Rating`\n+One player can have many ratings. Each rating belongs to exactly one player.\n+\n+- `Match` 1-N `Rating`\n+One match can have many ratings. Each rating belongs to exactly one match.\n+\n+- `User` 1-N `Rating`\n+One user can create many ratings. Each rating belongs to exactly one user.\n+\n+## Foreign Key Overview\n+\n+| Entity | Foreign key | References |\n+| --- | --- | --- |\n+| `Club` | `LeagueId` | `League.Id` |\n+| `Player` | `ClubId` | `Club.Id` |\n+| `Match` | `LeagueId` | `League.Id` |\n+| `Match` | `HomeTeamId` | `Club.Id` |\n+| `Match` | `AwayTeamId` | `Club.Id` |\n+| `Rating` | `PlayerId` | `Player.Id` |\n+| `Rating` | `MatchId` | `Match.Id` |\n+| `Rating` | `UserId` | `User.Id` |\n+\n+## Notes\n+\n+- The model uses EF Core annotations such as `Key`, `Required`, `MaxLength`, `ForeignKey`, and `InverseProperty`.\n+- `Position` is an enum in the domain model, not a separate entity table.\n+- `Match` contains two separate references to `Club`, so home and away relationships are explicitly modeled.\n*** End Patch"},"tool_use_id":"call_pltMxUFlsPxfJa6wvqvjyS2Q__vscode-1777798830827","cwd":"c:\\Users\\Fran\\Desktop\\C#\\FutScores"}
